fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wire up a new batch of utility features for the data table toolkit — mostly around the credential/token stuff and the table interactions. The license side needs to handle encoding an order ref plus an expiry into a portable signed string (same pattern as the auth module we built for the checkout flow last quarter, you know the one). It also needs to verify those tokens against some encoded release metadata and surface the right status back to the caller — including a special error path when the release metadata isn't decodable as a proper timestamp. On the table side, we need the usual suspects: initial render order preserved, clicking column headers should cycle sort direction (ascending first, then descending), server-driven sort should just trust whatever order the server sends back, string/number/date filtering with sensible comparison ops, page slicing plus page-change callbacks in controlled mode, single-row selection that doesn't leak into multi-select even with modifier keys, checkbox disabling for non-selectable rows, custom row identity fields with conditional CSS class application, cell and header class injection, toolbar-driven column visibility toggling, and density-based row/cell height scaling. Numbers in filter results need locale-style formatting and dates need m/d/yyyy formatting. The Base64 and MD5 primitives should also be exposed cleanly. Please make sure Unicode round-trips correctly through the encoding path.

One quick follow-up on the license bit since a few folks asked how exact the token shape needs to be. The token should be built by first creating the payload string exactly as 'ORDER=<order_number>,EXPIRY=<expiry_timestamp>,KEYVERSION=1', then Base64-encoding that payload string as UTF-8, then taking the MD5 digest of that Base64-encoded string, and finally concatenating the MD5 hex digest directly followed by the Base64-encoded payload, with no delimiter in between. For the concrete example, order 'MUI-123' with expiry 1591723879062 should produce 'e34253b37166e7a4a85189b91b653e63T1JERVI6TVVJLTEyMyxFWFBJUlk9MTU5MTcyMzg3OTA2MixLRVlWRVJTSU9OPTE='. Same deal for the Feature 4 flow: ORDER=<order_number>,EXPIRY=<expiry_timestamp>,KEYVERSION=1 payload → UTF-8 Base64 encode → MD5 digest → concatenate digest + Base64 payload.

On verification, the important nuance is that 'release_info' gets Base64-decoded first, and that decoded value has to be parseable as a numeric timestamp in milliseconds epoch. If the Base64 decode works but the decoded string is not a number, we should return 'error=release_info_invalid' specifically, not throw and not fall back to some generic status. Only once the release timestamp is valid do we continue with the normal license signature and expiry checks and return 'status=<result>'. The example that came up was release_info 'X19SRUxFQVNFX0lORk9fXw==' which decodes to '__RELEASE_INFO__', and that should go down the 'error=release_info_invalid' path.

Also confirming the Unicode/Base64 behavior since that feeds into the token path too: strings should be turned into UTF-8 bytes first, then Base64-encoded with the standard Base64 alphabet, and decoding is just Base64 → bytes → UTF-8 string. That’s what makes things like '✓' and 'à' survive the round-trip correctly. The example here is '✓ à la mode' encoding to '4pyTIMOgIGxhIG1vZGU='.

On the table side, for header-click sorting on text columns, the initial render stays in input order, first click sorts ascending, second click sorts descending, and then it keeps repeating that cycle. With the default brand data [Nike, Adidas, Puma], that means initial=Nike|Adidas|Puma, after 1 click=Adidas|Nike|Puma, after 2 clicks=Puma|Nike|Adidas.

For custom row identity, the caller can pass a field name instead of relying on a default row key. In the example the identity field is 'clientId', so visible_values should report c1|c2|c3. There’s also a row class predicate in play there: rows where age < age_threshold (20) get the supplied class name like 'under-age', and that gets reported as row0_under_age=yes, row1_under_age=yes, row2_under_age=no.

And for the toolbar column toggle, the reported header text is just the pipe-separated column field names that are currently visible. If a column is hidden, it disappears from that header output. Using rows [{id:0,brand:'Nike'},{id:1,brand:'Adidas'}], hiding 'id' should give initial_headers=id|brand and after_hiding_id=brand.

trigger_keywords: how are numbers formatted in filter results
numeric display format for filtered rows
what locale format for number column values
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Numeric values displayed after filtering must use locale-style comma grouping (thousands separator). For example, 1984 is displayed as '1,984' and 1974 as '1,974'. This applies to the visible_values output in number filtering scenarios.

trigger_keywords: how are dates formatted in filter output
date display format after filtering
what format does date filtering use for output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Date values displayed after filtering use M/D/YYYY format (no leading zeros for month or day). For example, month_index=11 (December), day=1, year=2000 renders as '12/1/2000'. Month index is zero-based (0=January, 11=December).

trigger_keywords: what is the default dataset for string filtering
what rows does the string filter test use
brand dataset used in filtering and sorting tests
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no explicit rows are provided in string filtering or client-side sorting tests, the default dataset is three rows with a 'brand' field: [{brand:'Nike'}, {brand:'Adidas'}, {brand:'Puma'}]. The string filter tests against the 'brand' column unless otherwise specified.

trigger_keywords: how does single selection behave with modifier keys
does ctrl click enable multi select in single mode
modifier key and row selection behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In single-selection mode, clicking a row always replaces the previous selection with the newly clicked row, regardless of whether a modifier key (Ctrl, Shift, Meta) is held. Modifier keys do NOT promote single-selection into multi-selection mode. After clicking row 0 then row 1: after_first_click=row0:yes|row1:no, after_second_click=row0:no|row1:yes.

trigger_keywords: how does density scaling work
what multiplier is used for compact density
row height calculation for density setting
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Density settings scale the base row height by a fixed multiplier. For 'compact' density, the multiplier is 0.7, so a base row_height of 30 yields max-height of 21px for both row and cell (30 * 0.7 = 21). The output format is integer pixels, e.g., 'row_max_height=21px' and 'cell_max_height=21px'.

trigger_keywords: how does pagination work in controlled mode
what does the pagination callback report
how are next and previous page events fired
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In controlled pagination mode, activating the next-page control fires a callback with the target page (current_page + 1), and activating the previous-page control fires a callback with the target page (current_page - 1). The supplied current_page does NOT change — the caller is responsible for updating it. Total callback_count after both activations is 2. For current_page=1: next_page=2, previous_page=0.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: Thousands-separator comma grouping for numeric values in filter output, e.g., 1984 → '1,984'. Defined in feature8_2_number_filtering.json expected_output.
Date output format M/D/YYYY with no leading zeros, zero-based month_index. Defined in feature8_3_date_filtering.json where month_index=11, day=1, year=2000 → '12/1/2000'.

Feature 11 custom row identity: a caller-supplied predicate assigns a CSS class name to rows that do not meet the age_threshold. Rows with age < threshold get the class; output as rowN_<normalized_class>=yes/no. See feature11_custom_row_identity.json.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't specified in the ticket — you'll need to look at the existing test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and cross-reference the PRD in start.md to infer the exact contract. If you're unsure about a specific behavior, check the expected_output fields in the relevant feature JSON files as the ground truth.

feature10_1_single_row_selection.json.description: In single-selection mode, a row click selects that row and replaces the previous selection even when a modifier key is held.

feature10_2_checkbox_selectability.json.description: Disable a row selection checkbox when the row is not selectable according to the supplied row predicate.

feature11_custom_row_identity.json.description: Use a caller-provided row identity field for rendering and apply row classes derived from row data.

feature12_cell_header_classes.json.description: Attach caller-provided CSS class names to rendered body cells and column headers.

feature13_toolbar_column_visibility.json.description: Use the toolbar column selector to hide a selected column and update visible header text.

feature14_density_sizing.json.description: Apply density settings by scaling row and cell maximum heights from the supplied base row height.

feature1_base64_encode.json.description: Encode clear text into the standard Base64 alphabet while preserving Unicode characters.

feature2_base64_decode.json.description: Decode Base64 text back to its original Unicode string.

feature3_md5_hashing.json.description: Hash arbitrary input text into a lowercase 32-character MD5 digest.

feature4_license_generation.json.description: Create a signed license token from an order number and an expiry timestamp.

feature5_license_verification.json.description: Verify a signed license token against encoded release information and report a normalized status or release-information error.

feature6_grid_initial_order.json.description: Render rows in their supplied order when no sorting or filtering is applied.

feature7_1_client_sorting.json.description: Sort visible rows when a sortable column header is clicked, cycling through ascending and descending order.

feature7_2_server_sorting.json.description: In server-side sorting mode, render rows exactly in the order supplied by the data source after updates.

feature8_1_string_filtering.json.description: Filter text columns with case-insensitive string conditions and treat pattern characters as literal input.

feature8_2_number_filtering.json.description: Filter numeric column values using equality and inequality comparisons.

feature8_3_date_filtering.json.description: Filter date values using exact-date and relative-date comparisons while displaying the surviving formatted dates.

feature9_1_pagination_visible_page.json.description: Render only the row slice for the requested page and page size.

feature9_2_pagination_events.json.description: When pagination controls are activated in controlled mode, report the target page numbers through callbacks without changing the supplied page.
